text: Cadaveric simulation in preparation for technical challenge. Complete portal robotic distal tracheal and left main stem resection and reconstruction on ECMO. The patient initially presented when she was fifteen years old with complete obstruction of her left main stem. The CT demonstrates complete atelectasis of the left lung with an endobronchial lesion in the proximal left main stem. She underwent endobronchial excision of a benign granular cell tumor with complete re-expansion of the lung but had persistent disease and over the course of the next year and a half demonstrated subtle increase in size. An additional perspective shows flattening of the aortic wall. We plan for a robotic reception of the left trachea and main stem bronchus with primary reconstruction.

The surgeon performs a cadaveric dissection, collapsing the right lung and separating the left lung while on ECMO. The subcranial space is dissected, with removal of subcranial lymph nodes, exposing the left atrium, left main stem bronchus, and the overlying esophagus. A Penrose drain is placed around the distal trachea. The left main stem bronchus is circumferentially dissected and transected from the trachea, with a portion resected in preparation for anastomosis.

The surgeon places a stay suture on the distal left main stem, but excessive traction causes avulsion of the cartilaginous rings. The right main stem is then cleared and transected just below the carina. A stay suture is placed on the distal right main stem, and the initial knot is tied using a barbed suture. The circumferential anastomosis is completed, and the two ends are ligated together.

The surgeon inspects the circumferential anastomosis and observes a technical error with the knot not being tight. The trachea is elevated, highlighted by an arrow. The pleura is opened underneath the azygos vein, and dissection occurs at the right tracheobronchial angle, with the vagus nerve highlighted by an arrow. The subcranial packet of lymph nodes is dissected to clear the subcranial space. Dissection continues on the left lateral side of the trachea, where the recurrent nerve is carefully separated from the airway.

The surgeon performs circumferential dissection on the distal trachea, passing umbilical tape around the airway for traction. In the subcranial space, the crina and left main stem are elevated to facilitate dissection posterior to the veins and anterior to the airway. A stay suture is placed on the medial aspect of the left main stem and through the tumor in the distal trachea, marking the beginning of the resection.

The surgeon completely divides the left main stem bronchus and resects additional margins of the anterior left main stem and distal trachea. A stay suture is placed on the left lateral aspect of the trachea and another on the left lateral aspect of the left main stem bronchus. A bronchotomy is performed in the bronchus intermedius, resecting additional cartilage segments to match the size of the left main stem bronchus. An end-side anastomosis is performed between the left main stem and the bronchus intermedius in a cartilaginous region to prevent collapse.

The surgeon uses two running sutures of 3-0 PDS to re-approximate the tracheotomy, starting at the cephalad portion and running to the inferior aspect. Once the sutures meet, they are tied to complete the airway reconstruction. The stay sutures on the left main stem and bronchus intermedius are tied together to relieve tension from the anastomosis. The lung is inflated to check for air leaks, and none are observed. A bronchoscopic view shows the tracheal anastomosis, the neocarina between the left main stem and bronchus intermedius, and the right upper lobe takeoff.

The patient was weaned from ECMO and extubated in the operating room. She was admitted to the ICU and transferred to the floor the next day. Drains were removed sequentially and she was discharged on hospital day six with this x-ray on discharge. She was seen for follow-up a week and a half following discharge. This is her left chest and this is her right chest. She was cleared to return to school. In summary, this video demonstrates the utility of cadaveric simulation in preparation for a novel procedure. The exercise allowed us to practice technical skills as well as identify potential opportunity for errors. We feel the exercise was critical to the success of this procedure.
